summary:
The Glorious Revolution (1688–1689 AD) was the largely bloodless overthrow of the Catholic King James II of England by a Protestant coalition that invited the Dutch Stadtholder William of Orange to invade, establishing constitutional monarchy and parliamentary supremacy as permanent features of the English state and laying the ideological foundations that would later inspire the [[event:106|American]] and [[event:107|French]] Revolutions. James II's short reign (1685–1688) had alarmed Protestant England by his pro-Catholic policies: dispensing with the Test Acts (which excluded Catholics from public office), building up an army, and — most alarmingly — producing a Catholic male heir in June 1688, threatening a permanent Catholic succession. A coalition of Whig and Tory nobles issued an invitation to William of Orange, husband of James's Protestant daughter Mary, to intervene.

William landed with a Dutch army in November 1688; James's army melted away and he fled to France (legally constituting an 'abdication' by desertion). Parliament declared the throne vacant and offered it jointly to William and Mary, subject to accepting the [[event:2409|Bill of Rights]] (1689) — which prohibited a Catholic monarch, guaranteed parliamentary control of taxation and legislation, forbade standing armies in peacetime without parliamentary consent, and secured certain individual rights. The Toleration Act (1689) extended limited religious freedom to Protestant nonconformists. The financial revolution that followed — the founding of the [[event:2511|Bank of England]] (1694), the development of government bonds — allowed Britain to mobilize unprecedented levels of credit for the wars against Louis XIV's France, turning the constitutional settlement into a geopolitical advantage. The Glorious Revolution's combination of political stability, limited government, and financial innovation is often cited as a key precondition for Britain's subsequent [[event:108|Industrial Revolution]].
